title: Results at a Glance - Evaluation of the National Collaborating Centres for Public Health Program, 2018-19 to 2022-23 - Canada.ca
content:
## Results at a Glance - Evaluation of the National Collaborating Centres for Public Health Program, 2018-19 to 2022-23
March 2024 Office of Audit and Evaluation
The National Collaborating Centres for Public Health (NCCPH) program was created in 2005 alongside the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) and the Pan-Canadian Public Health Network (PHN) in order to support evidence-informed decision making at all levels of Canada's public health system. The six National Collaborating Centres (NCCs) fulfill this mission by 1) identifying knowledge gaps, 2) bringing together stakeholders to work collaboratively, and 3) creating and disseminating knowledge translation resources.
Each NCC specializes in a different priority area of public health practice but share the same model: each are hosted in a university or provincial public health agency, supported with contribution agreement funding from PHAC, led and staffed by subject-matter experts, and guided by a stakeholder advisory board.
Since 2017 the Program has been managed by the Office of the Chief Science Officer (OCSO) which acts as a 'single window' for PHAC-NCC collaborations and administers the six contribution agreements.
- Core PHAC funding for the NCCPH program: $974K/year/NCC from 2018 to 2023 ($29.2M total over five years).
- PHAC provided short-term funding averaging $1.5M per NCC over five years for the COVID-19 response and specific projects.
- NCCs received $5.0M in external funding over the last five years.
This evaluation examined the relevance, effectiveness and efficiency of PHAC's NCCPH program from 2018-19 to 2022-23.
- Key informant interviews
- Performance data review
- Supplementary key informant questionnaire
- Financial data review

## What the evaluation found
The NCCs have continued to perform well, functioning as designed, leveraging their strengths and networks to support the knowledge needs of a wide variety of stakeholders, including PHAC, via their three core functions. Collectively, the NCCs produced hundreds of knowledge products and activities over the five years and supported more than 300 organizations in 2022-23. This included pivoting from their established work plans to support the COVID-19 response of PHAC and other stakeholders. They continue to provide leadership on issues like health equity, Reconciliation and climate change. They have also increasingly collaborated with PHAC units, notably on the Chief Public Health Officer's annual reports.
The work of the NCCs has been facilitated by their maturity, expertise, credibility with stakeholders across jurisdictions, and adaptability to new public health priorities. The NCC model, including the contribution agreement mechanism, appears to be a key factor in promoting these strengths.
The NCCs have been facing increasing challenges in meeting stakeholder expectations without supplementary funding from PHAC and external funders. However, supplementary funding impacts annual workplan commitments and does not address long-term capacity. The NCCs are approaching a point where core funding will no longer be sufficient to retain enough experienced staff to be effective. This has led to uncertainty regarding PHAC's expectations for this long-standing program, specifically what constitutes success in fulfilling its mission.
The PHAC-NCC relationship is mutually supportive, with OCSO facilitating PHAC-NCC collaborations. However, the strategic potential for two-way sharing on stakeholder needs and priorities remains unrealized. In addition, the performance measurement data is difficult to use to report on program results.
- Reassess PHAC expectations of the NCCPH Program in light of funding, PHAC and public health renewal.
- Enhance the two-way information-sharing relationship between PHAC and the NCCs.
- Improve the NCCPH performance measurement framework to better tell the Program results and impacts story.
